title: MLPC Exam 2023 Retry — JKU
description: Retake exam covering core MLPC topics

questions.context: Bayesian Classification. Consider minimum risk classification as introduced in the lecture, with the Conditional Risk formula: $$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$$ Assume we have \(\Omega = \{\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_c\}\), a list of actions \(\{\alpha_i = \text{"predict class } i\text{"}\}\), a given cost function \(\Lambda = \{\lambda_{ij} = \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)\}\), and known probability distributions \(P(\Omega)\) and \(P(X \mid \Omega)\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: MAP classification behaviour can be obtained by an appropriate setting of the cost function.
questions.explanation: Setting λ_ij = 0 for i=j and λ_ij = 1 for i≠j (the 0-1 loss) reduces minimum-risk to MAP classification.

questions.context: Bayesian Classification. Consider minimum risk classification as introduced in the lecture, with the Conditional Risk formula: $$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$$ Assume we have \(\Omega = \{\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_c\}\), a list of actions \(\{\alpha_i = \text{"predict class } i\text{"}\}\), a given cost function \(\Lambda = \{\lambda_{ij} = \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)\}\), and known probability distributions \(P(\Omega)\) and \(P(X \mid \Omega)\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: The effect of any change to the cost function can also be achieved by leaving the cost function unchanged and modifying the likelihoods \(P(X \mid \omega_j)\) accordingly.
questions.explanation: The cost function changes decision thresholds; this cannot generally be replicated by modifying P(X|ω_j), which are fixed likelihood functions determined by the data.

questions.context: Bayesian Classification. Consider minimum risk classification as introduced in the lecture, with the Conditional Risk formula: $$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$$ Assume we have \(\Omega = \{\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_c\}\), a list of actions \(\{\alpha_i = \text{"predict class } i\text{"}\}\), a given cost function \(\Lambda = \{\lambda_{ij} = \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)\}\), and known probability distributions \(P(\Omega)\) and \(P(X \mid \Omega)\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: Written as a matrix, the above cost function \(\Lambda\) is of quadratic shape.
questions.explanation: There are c possible actions and c possible classes, so Λ has c rows and c columns — a c×c square (quadratic) matrix.

questions.context: Bayesian Classification. Consider minimum risk classification as introduced in the lecture, with the Conditional Risk formula: $$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$$ Assume we have \(\Omega = \{\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_c\}\), a list of actions \(\{\alpha_i = \text{"predict class } i\text{"}\}\), a given cost function \(\Lambda = \{\lambda_{ij} = \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)\}\), and known probability distributions \(P(\Omega)\) and \(P(X \mid \Omega)\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: For any cost function, the expected cost is equal to the Bayes Error.
questions.explanation: Bayes error is the minimum achievable error under the 0-1 loss. Under a different cost function, the expected risk has a different numerical value and interpretation.

questions.context: Bayesian Classification. Consider minimum risk classification as introduced in the lecture, with the Conditional Risk formula: $$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$$ Assume we have \(\Omega = \{\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_c\}\), a list of actions \(\{\alpha_i = \text{"predict class } i\text{"}\}\), a given cost function \(\Lambda = \{\lambda_{ij} = \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)\}\), and known probability distributions \(P(\Omega)\) and \(P(X \mid \Omega)\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: The expected risk defines decision boundaries only in a two-dimensional feature space.
questions.explanation: Minimum-risk decision boundaries are defined wherever two conditional risks are equal, which can occur in any feature space dimensionality.

questions.context: Bayesian Classification / Minimum Risk. Consider the same setup as above with cost function \(\Lambda\) and conditional risk \(R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j \lambda_{ij} P(\omega_j \mid x)\).
questions.q: If we multiply the cost function with a constant \(k\), the probability of all classes will (multiple answers possible):
opts: change so that probable classes become less probable, and vice versa ("smoothing")
questions.explanation: Multiplying costs by a constant rescales risks uniformly but does not affect class posterior probabilities.

questions.context: Bayesian Classification / Minimum Risk. Consider the same setup as above with cost function \(\Lambda\) and conditional risk \(R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j \lambda_{ij} P(\omega_j \mid x)\).
questions.q: If we set the cost function to all 1 (i.e., all \(\lambda_{ij} = 1\)), then (multiple answers possible):
opts: minimum risk classification becomes the same as MAP classification
the risk for all actions becomes \(c\), for any \(x\), where \(c > 1\) is the number of classes
all decisions will have the same cost, for any \(x\)
the risk for all actions becomes 1, for any \(x\)
for any \(x\), every decision is equally good
questions.explanation: With all \(\lambda_{ij}=1\): \(R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1\) for every action, so all decisions have equal cost and are equally good.

questions.context: Classifier Evaluation. Consider a two-class classification problem with classes p and n, and a test set T with 1000 test examples and class distribution P:N = 3:7. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: The iso-performance line cannot go through points (0, 0) and (1, 1).
questions.explanation: The diagonal line TPR = FPR passes through (0,0) and (1,1) and represents a random classifier — it is a valid iso-performance line.

questions.context: Classifier Evaluation. Consider a two-class classification problem with classes p and n, and a test set T with 1000 test examples and class distribution P:N = 3:7. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: For any classifier C and any dataset D, \(\text{Recall}(C, D) + \text{Precision}(C, D) = 1.0\).
questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) and Recall = TP/(TP+FN) are independent quantities; they generally do not sum to 1.

questions.context: Classifier Evaluation. Consider a two-class classification problem with classes p and n, and a test set T with 1000 test examples and class distribution P:N = 3:7. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: If two classifiers \(C_1, C_2\) have the same True Positive Rates (TPRs), but different False Positive Rates, they cannot have the same accuracy on any given fixed test set T.
questions.explanation: Accuracy = (TP + TN)/(P+N). With fixed TPR = TP/P, different FPR changes TN = N·(1−FPR), hence changing total correct predictions and accuracy.

questions.context: Classifier Evaluation. Consider a two-class classification problem with classes p and n, and a test set T with 1000 test examples and class distribution P:N = 3:7. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: Two classifiers with the same accuracy cannot occupy different places in ROC space.
questions.explanation: Different (TPR, FPR) pairs can yield the same accuracy on an imbalanced dataset; accuracy is constant along iso-performance lines, which cross multiple ROC positions.

questions.context: Decision Trees. Consider the following alternative criterion (instead of information gain) for evaluating a feature \(A\) for splitting in the ID3 algorithm, in a binary classification task with two classes \(\Omega = \{+, -\}\) and \(p_i\) (\(n_i\)) = number of pos (neg) examples in \(D_i\), the \(i\)-th branch: $$G_D(A) = \sum_{v_i \in A} \frac{|D_i|}{|D|} \cdot \frac{|p_i - n_i|}{p_i + n_i}$$
questions.q: The best feature is the one that minimises this measure.
questions.explanation: Pure branches yield \(|p_i - n_i|/(p_i+n_i) = 1\); the measure is maximised for purer splits, so the best feature maximises it.

questions.context: Decision Trees. Consider the following alternative criterion (instead of information gain) for evaluating a feature \(A\) for splitting in the ID3 algorithm, in a binary classification task with two classes \(\Omega = \{+, -\}\) and \(p_i\) (\(n_i\)) = number of pos (neg) examples in \(D_i\), the \(i\)-th branch: $$G_D(A) = \sum_{v_i \in A} \frac{|D_i|}{|D|} \cdot \frac{|p_i - n_i|}{p_i + n_i}$$
questions.q: This measure will become maximal when each branch only contains examples of one class.
questions.explanation: When a branch is pure, |p_i − n_i|/(p_i+n_i) = 1; the weighted sum is maximised at 1.0 — consistent with the note that G_D is already documented to equal 1.0 for a perfect feature.

questions.context: Decision Trees. Consider the following alternative criterion (instead of information gain) for evaluating a feature \(A\) for splitting in the ID3 algorithm, in a binary classification task with two classes \(\Omega = \{+, -\}\) and \(p_i\) (\(n_i\)) = number of pos (neg) examples in \(D_i\), the \(i\)-th branch: $$G_D(A) = \sum_{v_i \in A} \frac{|D_i|}{|D|} \cdot \frac{|p_i - n_i|}{p_i + n_i}$$
questions.q: When a feature \(A\) perfectly discriminates between the classes in \(D\), \(G_D(A)\):
opts: is equal to the information gain
questions.explanation: When \(A\) perfectly separates classes, every branch has either all \(+\) or all \(-\) examples, so each \(|p_i-n_i|/(p_i+n_i)=1\) and the weighted sum equals 1.

questions.context: Decision Trees — ID3-S variant. Consider a variant "ID3-S" of the ID3 algorithm that learns only trees that are at most one level deep. It chooses a feature for the root, using the Information Gain heuristic, and labels each of the resulting nodes with the class that is most frequent in that node (in case of a tie, it randomly chooses between one of the competing classes). Which of the following statements about ID3-S are true:
questions.q: This is not a valid learning algorithm.
questions.explanation: ID3-S terminates and produces a consistent classifier (depth-1 decision stump); it is a valid, if restricted, learning algorithm.

questions.context: Decision Trees — ID3-S variant. Consider a variant "ID3-S" of the ID3 algorithm that learns only trees that are at most one level deep. It chooses a feature for the root, using the Information Gain heuristic, and labels each of the resulting nodes with the class that is most frequent in that node (in case of a tie, it randomly chooses between one of the competing classes). Which of the following statements about ID3-S are true:
questions.q: This algorithm cannot result in a classifier with an accuracy of 1.0 on the training set.
questions.explanation: If a single feature perfectly separates all training examples into pure branches, the depth-1 tree achieves 100% training accuracy.

questions.context: Decision Trees — ID3-S variant. Consider a variant "ID3-S" of the ID3 algorithm that learns only trees that are at most one level deep. It chooses a feature for the root, using the Information Gain heuristic, and labels each of the resulting nodes with the class that is most frequent in that node (in case of a tie, it randomly chooses between one of the competing classes). Which of the following statements about ID3-S are true:
questions.q: This is a high-bias learner.
questions.explanation: Limiting depth to 1 severely restricts the hypothesis space — the learner cannot express non-linear or multi-feature patterns, which is the definition of high inductive bias.

questions.context: Decision Trees — ID3-S variant. Consider a variant "ID3-S" of the ID3 algorithm that learns only trees that are at most one level deep. It chooses a feature for the root, using the Information Gain heuristic, and labels each of the resulting nodes with the class that is most frequent in that node (in case of a tie, it randomly chooses between one of the competing classes). Which of the following statements about ID3-S are true:
questions.q: Combining different such trees via voting can improve accuracy on some dataset.
questions.explanation: Ensembles of weak learners (stumps) can overcome individual bias via voting, as demonstrated by AdaBoost and random forests of decision stumps.

questions.context: Neural Networks and Deep Learning. Consider the simple neural network from the lecture slides, with 1 input unit (for a feature \(x\)), one hidden unit, and one output unit \(y\), where the hidden unit uses tanh as activation function, and the output unit \(y\) uses the identity function (i.e., just outputs what comes in as net input). Also assume that the loss function is the sum squared error. Which of the following statements are correct for this network?
questions.q: A training set for this network will have to consist of pairs of numbers.
questions.explanation: The network maps a scalar input x to a scalar output y, so each training example is an (x, y) pair of numbers.

questions.context: Neural Networks and Deep Learning. Consider the simple neural network from the lecture slides, with 1 input unit (for a feature \(x\)), one hidden unit, and one output unit \(y\), where the hidden unit uses tanh as activation function, and the output unit \(y\) uses the identity function (i.e., just outputs what comes in as net input). Also assume that the loss function is the sum squared error. Which of the following statements are correct for this network?
questions.q: Changing the output activation function from linear (identity) to tanh will not make any difference, as the hidden unit already uses the tanh activation.
questions.explanation: The identity output is unbounded; replacing it with tanh restricts predictions to (−1, 1) and changes gradients during backpropagation — a significant difference.

questions.context: Neural Networks and Deep Learning. Consider the simple neural network from the lecture slides, with 1 input unit (for a feature \(x\)), one hidden unit, and one output unit \(y\), where the hidden unit uses tanh as activation function, and the output unit \(y\) uses the identity function (i.e., just outputs what comes in as net input). Also assume that the loss function is the sum squared error. Which of the following statements are correct for this network?
questions.q: If we change the output of the bias unit from 1.0 to 2.0, the result is no longer a valid neural network.
questions.explanation: The bias output value is a design choice; 2.0 is equivalent to a standard bias with its weights halved. The network remains fully valid.

questions.context: Neural Networks and Deep Learning. Consider the simple neural network from the lecture slides, with 1 input unit (for a feature \(x\)), one hidden unit, and one output unit \(y\), where the hidden unit uses tanh as activation function, and the output unit \(y\) uses the identity function (i.e., just outputs what comes in as net input). Also assume that the loss function is the sum squared error. Which of the following statements are correct for this network?
questions.q: Changing the output of the bias unit from 1.0 to 2.0 will change the absolute value of the Sum Squared Error on the training set \(D\), but will not change the values of the network weights that minimise the error on \(D\).
questions.explanation: With bias output 2.0, the effective bias contribution doubles for any given weight; optimal weights that minimise SSE will therefore be different (halved bias weights achieve the same fit).

questions.context: Neural Networks and Deep Learning. Consider the simple neural network from the lecture slides, with 1 input unit (for a feature \(x\)), one hidden unit, and one output unit \(y\), where the hidden unit uses tanh as activation function, and the output unit \(y\) uses the identity function (i.e., just outputs what comes in as net input). Also assume that the loss function is the sum squared error. Which of the following statements are correct for this network?
questions.q: The loss function is defined over a four-dimensional parameter space.
questions.explanation: Parameters: input-to-hidden weight, hidden bias, hidden-to-output weight, output bias — four total.

questions.context: Neural Networks — Binary Classification. Now assume we use the above simple network for a binary classification task. We encode the class labels as 1 and 0, equip the output unit \(y\) with a logistic activation function, and use the Binary Cross-Entropy as the loss function: $$CE(x_i) = -y_i \log \hat{y}_i - (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i)$$ where \(y_i\) is the true class of example \(x_i\) (0 or 1), and \(\hat{y}_i\) is the value predicted by output unit \(y\). An example \(x_i\) is classified as class 1 iff \(\hat{y}_i \geq 0.5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: This classifier can only learn decision boundaries that split the feature space into two regions.
questions.explanation: The single logistic output with threshold ŷ ≥ 0.5 defines one decision boundary, partitioning the feature space into exactly two regions.

questions.context: Neural Networks — Binary Classification. Now assume we use the above simple network for a binary classification task. We encode the class labels as 1 and 0, equip the output unit \(y\) with a logistic activation function, and use the Binary Cross-Entropy as the loss function: $$CE(x_i) = -y_i \log \hat{y}_i - (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i)$$ where \(y_i\) is the true class of example \(x_i\) (0 or 1), and \(\hat{y}_i\) is the value predicted by output unit \(y\). An example \(x_i\) is classified as class 1 iff \(\hat{y}_i \geq 0.5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: If we make the network deeper by adding additional hidden layers, the CE function must be extended with corresponding additional terms.
questions.explanation: Binary cross-entropy is defined only on the final output prediction ŷ_i and the true label y_i; adding hidden layers does not add terms to the CE formula.

questions.context: Neural Networks — Binary Classification. Now assume we use the above simple network for a binary classification task. We encode the class labels as 1 and 0, equip the output unit \(y\) with a logistic activation function, and use the Binary Cross-Entropy as the loss function: $$CE(x_i) = -y_i \log \hat{y}_i - (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i)$$ where \(y_i\) is the true class of example \(x_i\) (0 or 1), and \(\hat{y}_i\) is the value predicted by output unit \(y\). An example \(x_i\) is classified as class 1 iff \(\hat{y}_i \geq 0.5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: If the class distribution in the training set is 50:50, the binary cross-entropy CE encourages the network to predict values close to 0.5.
questions.explanation: CE is minimised when ŷ_i matches the true label (0 or 1). With balanced data, the network learns to predict close to 1 for class-1 inputs and close to 0 for class-0 inputs.

questions.context: Neural Networks — Binary Classification. Now assume we use the above simple network for a binary classification task. We encode the class labels as 1 and 0, equip the output unit \(y\) with a logistic activation function, and use the Binary Cross-Entropy as the loss function: $$CE(x_i) = -y_i \log \hat{y}_i - (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i)$$ where \(y_i\) is the true class of example \(x_i\) (0 or 1), and \(\hat{y}_i\) is the value predicted by output unit \(y\). An example \(x_i\) is classified as class 1 iff \(\hat{y}_i \geq 0.5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: This classifier can easily overfit because the cross-entropy is non-linear.
questions.explanation: Overfitting is caused by too many free parameters relative to training data, not by the non-linearity of the loss function.

questions.context: Unsupervised Learning / k-means. Consider the task of k-means clustering with \(N\) observations described in terms of \(D\) numeric features, with the Euclidean distance as distance measure. Assume we have set the number \(k\) of clusters to \(k = 5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: When we increase the number of features, the model complexity penalty term in the Bayes Information Criterion will automatically grow larger.
questions.explanation: BIC penalises the number of free parameters p. Each GMM component has a D-dimensional mean vector, so p grows with D, increasing the BIC penalty.

questions.context: Unsupervised Learning / k-means. Consider the task of k-means clustering with \(N\) observations described in terms of \(D\) numeric features, with the Euclidean distance as distance measure. Assume we have set the number \(k\) of clusters to \(k = 5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: In k-means, \(k\) has to be at least \(N/2\).
questions.explanation: k can be any value from 1 to N; there is no lower bound based on the data size.

questions.context: Unsupervised Learning / k-means. Consider the task of k-means clustering with \(N\) observations described in terms of \(D\) numeric features, with the Euclidean distance as distance measure. Assume we have set the number \(k\) of clusters to \(k = 5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: To use the Bayes Information Criterion for selecting an appropriate value for \(k\), a k-means cluster model must be converted into a probabilistic model.
questions.explanation: BIC is defined for probabilistic (likelihood-based) models. Pure k-means has no likelihood; it must be reinterpreted as a Gaussian mixture model to compute P(data | model).

questions.context: Unsupervised Learning / k-means. Consider the task of k-means clustering with \(N\) observations described in terms of \(D\) numeric features, with the Euclidean distance as distance measure. Assume we have set the number \(k\) of clusters to \(k = 5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: The cluster with the smallest number of members will have the lowest (best) MQE.
questions.explanation: MQE = mean squared distance to centroid. A small cluster with spread-out members can have higher MQE than a large, compact cluster.

questions.context: Unsupervised Learning / k-means. Consider the task of k-means clustering with \(N\) observations described in terms of \(D\) numeric features, with the Euclidean distance as distance measure. Assume we have set the number \(k\) of clusters to \(k = 5\). Which of the following statements are correct?
questions.q: Feature normalisation will change the MQE, but not the clusters found.
questions.explanation: Normalisation changes Euclidean distances, which alters which centroid each point is closest to — changing both the cluster assignments and the MQE.

questions.context: Unsupervised Learning — Gaussian Mixture Models. Consider a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with \(k\) components that was learned from a dataset of \(N\) examples over a \(D\)-dimensional numeric feature space.
questions.q: How many parameters (numbers) does a specification of this GMM consist of (ignoring possible redundancies because of distribution constraints and symmetries)?
questions.explanation: \(k\) mixture weights + \(kD\) mean parameters + \(kD^2\) covariance-matrix entries.

questions.context: Markov Models. Consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$ Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: If we changed some \(a_{ij}\) to be zero (0.0), then also \(a_{ji}\) would have to be changed to zero for a valid Markov process.
questions.explanation: Transition probabilities are not required to be symmetric. a_ij = 0 means state i cannot transition to j; a_ji can remain positive without violating any Markov property.

questions.context: Markov Models. Consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$ Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: If we made one of these numbers smaller, then at least one number in the same column would have to become larger.
questions.explanation: Rows of \(A\) must sum to 1 (not columns), so the constraint is on rows.

questions.context: Markov Models. Consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$ Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: If we made one of these numbers smaller, then at least one number in the same row would have to become larger.
questions.explanation: Rows of a Markov transition matrix must sum to 1, so decreasing one entry requires increasing at least one other entry in the same row.

questions.context: Markov Models. Consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$ Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: The expected number of consecutive occurrences of state S2 is higher than the expected number of consecutive occurrences of state S1.
questions.explanation: Self-loop probabilities: \(a_{22} = 0.6 > a_{11} = 0.4\), so expected run length in S2 is larger.

questions.context: Markov Models — Identity transition. Now consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ What is the probability of the following state sequences, according to this model \(M\)?
questions.explanation: With identity transition matrix, transitioning from S1 to S2 has probability 0, so the whole sequence has probability 0.

questions.context: Markov Models — Identity transition. Now consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ What is the probability of the following state sequences, according to this model \(M\)?
questions.explanation: \(\pi_1 = 1/3\) and each self-transition has probability 1.

questions.context: Markov Models — Identity transition. Now consider a simple Markov process with 3 states (S1, S2, S3), the following state transition model and a uniform prior state distribution \(\pi = [1/3, 1/3, 1/3]\): $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ What is the probability of the following state sequences, according to this model \(M\)?
questions.explanation: Any transition between distinct states has probability 0.

questions.context: Markov Models — Two-City Classification. Consider the classification task of identifying one of two cities A or B, based on a sequence of weather observations. We have learned two Markov models \(M_A\) and \(M_B\) that model the weather in cities A and B, respectively. Now, given a sequence \(S\) of 10 consecutive weather observations, you want to decide which of the two cities this sequence is from, based on the MAP principle.
questions.q: Which of the following quantities are needed for making this decision?
questions.explanation: MAP: choose argmax of \(P(M_c) P(S \mid M_c)\) for \(c \in \{A, B\}\).

questions.context: Markov Models — Two-City Classification. Consider the classification task of identifying one of two cities A or B, based on a sequence of weather observations. We have learned two Markov models \(M_A\) and \(M_B\) that model the weather in cities A and B, respectively. Now, given a sequence \(S\) of 10 consecutive weather observations, you want to decide which of the two cities this sequence is from, based on the MAP principle. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: If we produce a new sequence \(S_2\) by concatenating two copies of \(S\), the likelihoods will change, but the MAP decision will always remain the same.
questions.explanation: P(S₂|M) includes an extra junction transition factor a_{s₁₀,s₁} that differs between M_A and M_B; this extra factor can flip the MAP decision even if the original S favoured one city.

questions.context: Markov Models — Two-City Classification. Consider the classification task of identifying one of two cities A or B, based on a sequence of weather observations. We have learned two Markov models \(M_A\) and \(M_B\) that model the weather in cities A and B, respectively. Now, given a sequence \(S\) of 10 consecutive weather observations, you want to decide which of the two cities this sequence is from, based on the MAP principle. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: This kind of classification procedure is only possible if the length of \(S\) is at least 2.
questions.explanation: Even a single-state sequence can be classified using the prior P(city) and the initial state probability π(s₁) from each model.

questions.context: Markov Models — Two-City Classification. Consider the classification task of identifying one of two cities A or B, based on a sequence of weather observations. We have learned two Markov models \(M_A\) and \(M_B\) that model the weather in cities A and B, respectively. Now, given a sequence \(S\) of 10 consecutive weather observations, you want to decide which of the two cities this sequence is from, based on the MAP principle. Which of the following statements are true?
questions.q: The same decision could be made without a Markov model, by counting the different states in \(S\).
questions.explanation: The Markov model captures transition probabilities (sequential structure). Two sequences with identical state counts but different orderings can have very different likelihoods under the model.

questions.explanation:
```python
def reverse(self):
    cur = self.head
    while cur is not None:
        cur.prev, cur.next = cur.next, cur.prev
        cur = cur.prev  # move forward (was next, now prev)
    self.head, self.tail = self.tail, self.head
```

Master Theorem: T(N)=a·T(N/b)+f(N)
- Case 1: f(N)=O(N^(log_b(a)−ε)) → Θ(N^log_b(a))
- Case 2: f(N)=Θ(N^log_b(a)) → Θ(N^log_b(a)·log N)
- Case 3: f(N)=Ω(N^(log_b(a)+ε)) → Θ(f(N))

questions.explanation:
T(N) = 5·T(N/5) + N:
```
a=5, b=5, f(N)=N
log_5(5) = 1
f(N) = N = N^1 = N^(log_5 5)
→ Case 2: T(N) = Θ(N·log N)
```
**Answer: T(N) = Θ(N log N)**

title: Statistics Exercise Exam — SS2020
description: Bayes' theorem, normal distribution, descriptive statistics with grouped data

questions.context: A spam filter learned that the word 'win' appears in 30% of spam mails. 0.9% of non-spam mail includes 'win'. 40% of all mails are spam.
questions.q: a) What is the probability that a randomly selected email contains the word 'win'? \(P(\text{win}) = ?\)
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- \(P(\text{win}|\text{spam}) = 0.30\)
- \(P(\text{win}|\text{no spam}) = 0.009\)
- \(P(\text{spam}) = 0.40\), \(P(\text{no spam}) = 0.60\)

**Step 2:** Apply the total probability theorem:
$$P(\text{win}) = P(\text{win}|\text{spam})\cdot P(\text{spam}) + P(\text{win}|\text{no spam})\cdot P(\text{no spam})$$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$P(\text{win}) = 0.30 \times 0.40 + 0.009 \times 0.60 = 0.12 + 0.0054 = 0.1254$$

questions.context: A spam filter learned that the word 'win' appears in 30% of spam mails. 0.9% of non-spam mail includes 'win'. 40% of all mails are spam. (Use \(P(\text{win}) = 0.1254\) from part a.)
questions.q: b) Given that an email contains the word 'win', what is the probability it is NOT spam? \(P(\text{no spam}|\text{win}) = ?\)
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Apply Bayes' theorem:
$$P(\text{no spam}|\text{win}) = \frac{P(\text{win}|\text{no spam})\cdot P(\text{no spam})}{P(\text{win})}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$= \frac{0.009 \times 0.60}{0.1254} = \frac{0.0054}{0.1254} \approx 0.043$$

questions.context: Steel bolt lengths follow a normal distribution \(X \sim N(\mu = 8.54\text{ cm},\, \sigma = 0.02\text{ cm})\). Specification requires bolts in the range 8.50 cm to 8.60 cm.
questions.q: a) What proportion of bolts have a length greater than 8.54 cm? \(P(X > 8.54) = ?\)
questions.explanation:
**Key insight:** The mean is \(\mu = 8.54\) cm.

By the symmetry of the normal distribution, exactly half of the distribution lies above the mean:
$$P(X > 8.54) = P(X > \mu) = 0.5$$

questions.context: Steel bolt lengths follow \(X \sim N(\mu = 8.54\text{ cm},\, \sigma = 0.02\text{ cm})\). Specification: 8.50 cm to 8.60 cm.
questions.q: b) What proportion of bolts are OUTSIDE the specification range \([8.50,\, 8.60]\)?
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Standardize the bounds:
$$z_1 = \frac{8.50 - 8.54}{0.02} = \frac{-0.04}{0.02} = -2$$
$$z_2 = \frac{8.60 - 8.54}{0.02} = \frac{0.06}{0.02} = 3$$

**Step 2:** Find the probability within spec:
$$P(8.50 < X < 8.60) = \Phi(3) - \Phi(-2) \approx 0.9987 - 0.0228 = 0.9759$$

**Step 3:** Outside = complement:
$$P(\text{outside}) = 1 - 0.9759 = 0.0241$$

questions.q: a) Calculate the arithmetic mean of the grouped BMI data.
b) Calculate the median of the grouped BMI data.
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Find the median class.
- \(N/2 = 25\)th value.
- Cumulative frequencies: \([16;20)\): 6; \([20;24)\): \(6+20=26\).
- The 25th value falls in the class \([20;24)\).

**Step 2:** Apply the median interpolation formula:
$$\text{Median} = e_{i-1} + \frac{d_i}{f_i}\cdot\left(\frac{N}{2} - F_{i-1}\right)$$
where \(e_{i-1}=20\), \(d_i=4\) (class width), \(f_i=20\), \(F_{i-1}=6\):
$$= 20 + \frac{4}{20}\cdot(25 - 6) = 20 + 0.2 \times 19 = 20 + 3.8 = 23.8$$

questions.q: c) Calculate the 30th percentile \(x_{0.3}\) (i.e., the value below which 30% of observations fall).
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Find the class containing the 30th percentile.
- Target rank: \(N \times 0.3 = 50 \times 0.3 = 15\)th value.
- Cumulative: \([16;20)\): 6; \([20;24)\): 26. The 15th value is in \([20;24)\).

**Step 2:** Apply the percentile interpolation formula:
$$x_{0.3} = e_{i-1} + \frac{d_i}{f_i}\cdot(N\cdot 0.3 - F_{i-1})$$
$$= 20 + \frac{4}{20}\cdot(15 - 6) = 20 + 0.2 \times 9 = 20 + 1.8 = 21.8$$

title: Statistics Exercise Exam — SS2021 Group 1
description: Normal distribution, probability theory, continuous random variables, linear regression, set theory

questions.context: The daily high temperature in a city follows a normal distribution \(X \sim N(\mu = 22^\circ\text{C},\, \sigma = 5^\circ\text{C})\).
questions.q: Calculate the following probabilities and the symmetrical interval containing 60% of temperatures.
questions.explanation:
**Part a)** Standardize:
$$z_1 = \frac{20-22}{5} = -0.4,\quad z_2 = \frac{27-22}{5} = 1.0$$
$$P(20 < X < 27) = \Phi(1.0) - \Phi(-0.4) = 0.8413 - 0.3446 = 0.4967 \approx 0.4968$$

**Part b)** By symmetry of the normal distribution:
$$P(X \leq \mu) = 0.5 \text{ always}$$

**Part c)** Standardize:
$$z = \frac{24-22}{5} = 0.4$$
$$P(X \geq 24) = 1 - \Phi(0.4) = 1 - 0.6554 = 0.3446$$

**Part d)** Find \(z\) such that \(P(-z < Z < z) = 0.60\):
$$P(Z < z) = 0.80 \Rightarrow z \approx 0.842$$
Interval: \([\mu - z\sigma,\, \mu + z\sigma] = [22 - 0.842\times5,\, 22 + 0.842\times5] = [17.79,\, 26.21]\)

questions.context: A kindergarten has 30 children: 10 blond, 4 red-haired, 16 dark-haired. Eye colors: 5 green, 2 hazel, 10 blue, 13 grey. Assume hair and eye color are independent.
questions.q: Calculate the following probabilities.

rows.label: b) P(green eyes OR red hair)
c) P(hazel eyes OR not dark hair)

questions.explanation:
**Part a)** Green and blue eyes are mutually exclusive:
$$P(\text{green} \cup \text{blue}) = \frac{5+10}{30} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.5$$

**Part b)** Using inclusion-exclusion (events independent):
$$P(\text{green}) = \frac{5}{30},\quad P(\text{red hair}) = \frac{4}{30}$$
$$P(\text{green} \cup \text{red hair}) = \frac{5}{30} + \frac{4}{30} - \frac{5}{30}\cdot\frac{4}{30} = \frac{9}{30} - \frac{20}{900} = 0.3 - 0.0222 = 0.2778$$

**Part c)** Using inclusion-exclusion (events independent):
$$P(\text{hazel}) = \frac{2}{30},\quad P(\text{not dark hair}) = \frac{14}{30}$$
$$P(\text{hazel} \cup \text{not dark}) = \frac{2}{30} + \frac{14}{30} - \frac{2}{30}\cdot\frac{14}{30} = \frac{16}{30} - \frac{28}{900} = 0.5333 - 0.0311 = 0.5022$$

questions.context: A continuous random variable \(X\) has PDF \(f(x) = kx + 0.5k\) for \(0 \leq x \leq 6\), and \(f(x)=0\) otherwise.
questions.q: Find k, the CDF F(x), and P(X ≥ 4).
questions.explanation:
**Part a)** Normalization condition \(\int_0^6 f(x)\,dx = 1\):
$$\int_0^6 (kx + 0.5k)\,dx = k\left[\frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x}{2}\right]_0^6 = k(18 + 3) = 21k = 1$$
$$\Rightarrow k = \frac{1}{21} \approx 0.0476$$

**Part b)** CDF:
$$F(x) = \int_0^x \left(\frac{t}{21} + \frac{0.5}{21}\right)dt = \frac{x^2}{42} + \frac{x}{42}$$

**Part c)**
$$P(X \geq 4) = 1 - F(4) = 1 - \left(\frac{16}{42} + \frac{4}{42}\right) = 1 - \frac{20}{42} = \frac{22}{42} = \frac{11}{21} \approx 0.5238$$

questions.q: a) Calculate the Bravais-Pearson correlation coefficient \(r\).
questions.explanation:
**Formula:**
$$r = \frac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(x) \cdot \text{Var}(y)}}$$

**Compute:**
$$r = \frac{23.5008}{\sqrt{172.09 \times 3.6902}} = \frac{23.5008}{\sqrt{634.95}} = \frac{23.5008}{25.198} \approx 0.9326$$

This indicates a strong positive linear relationship between temperature and length.

questions.context: Copper rod regression: \(\bar{x}=37.58\), \(\bar{y}=464.02\), \(\text{Cov}=23.5008\), \(\text{Var}(x)=172.09\).
questions.q: b) Using the simple linear regression model \(\hat{y} = a + bx\), predict the rod length at \(x = 65^\circ\text{C}\).

questions.context:
Let A = 'second-hand car' and B = 'Japanese car'. It is known that:
- \(P(A \cup B) = 0.45\)
- \(P(A) = 0.25\)
- \(P(B) = 0.30\)
questions.q: Find \(P(A \cap B)\), the probability that a car is both second-hand AND Japanese.
questions.explanation:
**Apply the inclusion-exclusion principle:**
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Solve for \(P(A \cap B)\):**
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cup B) = 0.25 + 0.30 - 0.45 = 0.10$$

title: Statistics Lecture Exam — SS2020
description: Descriptive statistics concepts, binomial distribution, measures of location, conditional probability, linear regression, confidence intervals

questions.q: Explain the difference between ordinal and quantitative (metric) attributes. Give one example of each.
questions.explanation:
**Ordinal attributes** have categories that can be ranked/ordered, but the distances between categories are not meaningful or equal. You can say one value is 'more than' another, but not 'how much more'.
- Example: Education level (primary < secondary < university); satisfaction rating (poor < fair < good < excellent).

**Quantitative (metric) attributes** have numerical values where differences and ratios are meaningful. The scale has a meaningful unit.
- Example: Height in cm, income in euros, temperature in Kelvin.

**Key distinction:** For ordinal data you can rank observations; for metric data you can perform arithmetic operations (mean, standard deviation, etc.).

questions.q: What is a Type I error and a Type II error in hypothesis testing?
questions.explanation:
**Type I Error (False Positive / α-error):**
Rejecting the null hypothesis \(H_0\) when it is actually true.
- Probability = significance level \(\alpha\) (e.g., 5%).
- Example: Concluding a drug is effective when it actually is not.

**Type II Error (False Negative / β-error):**
Failing to reject \(H_0\) when the alternative hypothesis \(H_1\) is actually true.
- Probability = \(\beta\); the power of the test is \(1 - \beta\).
- Example: Concluding a drug is not effective when it actually is.

**Memory aid:** Type I = 'crying wolf' (falsely alarming); Type II = 'missing the wolf' (failing to detect).

questions.q: What is meant by 'confidence level' (e.g., 95%) in the context of confidence intervals?
questions.explanation:
A **confidence level of 95%** means: if we were to repeat the sampling procedure many times and construct a confidence interval each time, then **95% of those intervals** would contain the true population parameter.

**Important:** It does NOT mean 'there is a 95% probability that the true parameter lies in this specific interval.' Once an interval is computed, the true value either is or is not in it.

**Formal definition:** The confidence level \(1-\alpha\) is the long-run proportion of confidence intervals (from repeated sampling) that capture the true parameter value.

questions.q: Which of the following statements is correct?
opts: The covariance has a range of 0 to +∞
The standard error is completely independent from sample size
The median is an appropriate measure of location for ordinal data
For nominal data, the mean is an appropriate summary statistic
questions.explanation:
**Answer: Option C — The median is an appropriate measure of location for ordinal data.**

- **Option A is wrong:** Covariance can be negative (when variables move in opposite directions). Its range is \((-\infty, +\infty)\).

- **Option B is wrong:** The standard error \(SE = \sigma/\sqrt{n}\) depends directly on sample size \(n\). Larger samples give smaller standard errors.

- **Option C is correct:** The median requires only ranked (ordinal) data — you need to find the 'middle' value, which is meaningful for ordinal scales.

- **Option D is wrong:** The mean requires metric (interval/ratio) data with meaningful numerical differences. For nominal categories (e.g., hair color), computing a mean is meaningless.

questions.context: An urn contains 5 red and 6 green balls. You draw 4 balls **with replacement**.
questions.q: What is the probability of drawing exactly 1 green ball? \(X \sim \text{Bin}(n,p)\)
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- \(n = 4\) draws, with replacement
- \(p = P(\text{green}) = 6/11\)
- \(X \sim \text{Bin}(4,\, 6/11)\)

**Step 2:** Apply the binomial formula:
$$P(X=1) = \binom{4}{1}\cdot\left(\frac{6}{11}\right)^1\cdot\left(\frac{5}{11}\right)^3$$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$= 4 \cdot \frac{6}{11} \cdot \frac{125}{1331} = 4 \cdot \frac{750}{14641} = \frac{3000}{14641} \approx 0.2049$$

questions.context: Six rock climbers recorded the following vertical distances climbed (in meters): 214, 179, 195, 222, 24, 178.
questions.q: a) Calculate the arithmetic mean and median of the climbing distances.

questions.context: Six rock climbers: distances = {214, 179, 195, 222, 24, 178}. Mean \(\bar{x} = 168.667\).
questions.q: b) Calculate the variance and standard deviation of the climbing distances.

questions.context: A shot is observed to miss the target. It is known that Sue hits with probability 90% (misses 10%) and Bob hits with probability 70% (misses 30%). Assume each shot is equally likely to be from Sue or Bob (50/50 prior).
questions.q: Given that the shot missed, what is the probability that Bob fired it? \(P(\text{Bob}|\text{miss}) = ?\)
questions.explanation:
**Apply Bayes' theorem** (equal priors: \(P(\text{Sue}) = P(\text{Bob}) = 0.5\)):

**Step 1:** Likelihoods:
- \(P(\text{miss}|\text{Sue}) = 0.10\)
- \(P(\text{miss}|\text{Bob}) = 0.30\)

**Step 2:** Total probability of miss:
$$P(\text{miss}) = 0.5 \times 0.10 + 0.5 \times 0.30 = 0.05 + 0.15 = 0.20$$

**Step 3:** Bayes:
$$P(\text{Bob}|\text{miss}) = \frac{P(\text{miss}|\text{Bob})\cdot P(\text{Bob})}{P(\text{miss})} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.5}{0.20} = \frac{0.15}{0.20} = 0.75$$

questions.context:
A study on TV watching time (h/day) and weight (kg) with \(n=15\) participants. Summary statistics:
- \(\bar{x}_{\text{TV}} = 4.733\) h, \(\bar{y}_{\text{weight}} = 52.4\) kg
- \(\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 42.933\), \(\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 2561.6\)
- \(\text{Cov}(x,y) = 15.973\)
questions.q: a) Show the variance of TV time and variance of weight.
questions.explanation:
**Formula:** \(\text{Var} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2\)

**TV time variance:**
$$\text{Var}_{\text{TV}} = \frac{42.933}{15} = 2.862$$

**Weight variance:**
$$\text{Var}_{\text{weight}} = \frac{2561.6}{15} = 170.773$$

questions.context: TV/weight regression: \(\bar{x}=4.733\), \(\bar{y}=52.4\), \(\text{Cov}=15.973\), \(\text{Var}(x)=2.862\).
questions.q: b) Using the linear regression model, predict the weight of someone who watches 6 hours of TV per day.
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Calculate slope:
$$b = \frac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{\text{Var}(x)} = \frac{15.973}{2.862} = 5.581$$

**Step 2:** Calculate intercept:
$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 52.4 - 5.581 \times 4.733 = 52.4 - 26.415 = 25.985$$

**Step 3:** Predict at \(x=6\):
$$\hat{y} = 25.985 + 5.581 \times 6 = 25.985 + 33.486 = 59.471 \text{ kg}$$

questions.context: A sample of 6 blood pressure measurements: 71, 73, 74, 82, 90, 60. The sample mean \(\bar{x} = 75\).
questions.q: a) Construct a 95% confidence interval for the population mean blood pressure.
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Compute sample standard deviation:
- Deviations from mean (75): \(-4, -2, -1, 7, 15, -15\)
- Sum of squares: \(16+4+1+49+225+225 = 520\)
- \(s^2 = 520/(6-1) = 104\); \(s = \sqrt{104} \approx 10.198\)

**Step 2:** t-critical value with \(df = n-1 = 5\), \(\alpha/2 = 0.025\):
$$t_{5,\,0.975} = 2.571$$

**Step 3:** Construct CI:
$$\bar{x} \pm t_{5,0.975} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = 75 \pm 2.571 \cdot \frac{10.198}{\sqrt{6}} = 75 \pm 2.571 \times 4.163 = 75 \pm 10.70$$
$$\Rightarrow [64.30,\; 85.70]$$

questions.context: Blood pressure sample (n=6): 71, 73, 74, 82, 90, 60. \(\bar{x}=75\), \(s \approx 10.198\). Test \(H_0: \mu = 70\) vs \(H_1: \mu \neq 70\) at significance level \(\alpha = 5\%\).
questions.q: b) Perform the t-test: calculate the test statistic, critical value, and state the decision.
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Compute the test statistic:
$$t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{75 - 70}{10.198/\sqrt{6}} = \frac{5}{4.163} \approx 1.201$$

**Step 2:** Critical value for two-sided test, \(df=5\), \(\alpha=0.05\):
$$t_{5,\,0.975} = 2.571$$

**Step 3:** Compare:
$$|t| = 1.201 < 2.571 = t_{\text{crit}}$$

**Decision:** Fail to reject \(H_0\). There is insufficient evidence to conclude that the mean blood pressure differs from 70.

title: Statistics Lecture Exam — SS2021 Group 2
description: Basic probability, conceptual statistics, total probability, regression, hypothesis testing, binomial distribution

questions.context: A storage room contains 50 paint cans: 10 white, 5 blue, 8 green, 3 yellow, 6 red, 10 orange, 8 grey. A can is chosen at random.
questions.q: What is the probability of selecting a blue, green, OR orange can?
questions.explanation:
These events are mutually exclusive (a can has one color):
$$P(\text{blue} \cup \text{green} \cup \text{orange}) = \frac{5 + 8 + 10}{50} = \frac{23}{50} = 0.46$$

questions.q: What is the difference between a population and a sample in statistics?
questions.explanation:
**Population:** The complete set of all individuals, objects, or measurements that share a common characteristic and are of interest in a study. Example: all adults in Austria.

**Sample:** A subset of the population selected for measurement and analysis. Example: 1000 randomly selected Austrian adults.

**Why we sample:** Measuring the entire population is often too expensive, time-consuming, or impossible. Statistical inference allows us to draw conclusions about the population from the sample.

**Key requirement:** The sample should be representative (ideally random) to avoid bias.

questions.q: What is the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics?
questions.explanation:
**Descriptive statistics** summarizes and describes the data at hand (the sample or population). Tools: mean, median, standard deviation, histograms, box plots. No generalization beyond the data.

**Inferential statistics** uses sample data to make inferences, predictions, or decisions about the larger population. Tools: hypothesis tests, confidence intervals, regression analysis.

**Example:** Computing the mean exam score of 30 students in class = descriptive. Estimating the mean exam score of all students nationwide from those 30 = inferential.

questions.q: What is the empirical rule (68-95-99.7 rule) for a normal distribution?
questions.explanation:
For a normally distributed variable \(X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\):

- **68%** of observations fall within \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\)
- **95%** of observations fall within \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\)
- **99.7%** of observations fall within \(\mu \pm 3\sigma\)

**Why it matters:** It allows quick probability estimates without looking up tables. For example, if heights follow \(N(170\text{ cm}, 10\text{ cm})\), then about 95% of people are between 150 cm and 190 cm.

questions.q: Explain what a p-value represents in hypothesis testing.
questions.explanation:
The **p-value** is the probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one computed from the data, **assuming the null hypothesis \(H_0\) is true**.

**Interpretation:**
- A small p-value (e.g., \(p < 0.05\)) means the observed data would be very unlikely under \(H_0\), providing evidence against \(H_0\).
- A large p-value means the data are consistent with \(H_0\).

**Decision rule:** Reject \(H_0\) if \(p \leq \alpha\) (significance level).

**Common misconception:** The p-value is NOT the probability that \(H_0\) is true. It is a conditional probability of the data given \(H_0\).

questions.q: What is the coefficient of determination R² in linear regression, and what does it tell us?
questions.explanation:
**\(R^2\)** (coefficient of determination) measures the proportion of variance in the dependent variable \(y\) that is explained by the linear regression model.

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{residual}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{total}}} = r^2 \quad (\text{in simple linear regression})$$

**Interpretation:**
- \(R^2 = 1\): Perfect fit, all variance explained.
- \(R^2 = 0\): The regression explains none of the variance.
- \(R^2 = 0.80\): 80% of the variability in \(y\) is explained by the model.

**Caution:** High \(R^2\) does not necessarily mean a good model (it can be inflated by adding predictors or by overfitting).

questions.context: A driver must pass through 3 radar trap locations: L1 (active 40% of the time), L2 (30%), L3 (50%). If the trap is active, the probability of being caught speeding is: 20% at L1, 10% at L2, 70% at L3. The driver passes through all three locations.
questions.q: What is the total probability of receiving a speeding ticket?
questions.explanation:
**Apply the total probability theorem** across all three locations:

$$P(\text{ticket}) = P(\text{active}_1)\cdot P(\text{caught}|\text{active}_1) + P(\text{active}_2)\cdot P(\text{caught}|\text{active}_2) + P(\text{active}_3)\cdot P(\text{caught}|\text{active}_3)$$

$$= 0.40 \times 0.20 + 0.30 \times 0.10 + 0.50 \times 0.70$$

$$= 0.08 + 0.03 + 0.35 = 0.46$$

questions.context:
A study of 100 guinea pigs records age (months) and weight (grams). Summary statistics:
- \(\sum x_i = 12776\), \(\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 54534\)
- \(\bar{y}_{\text{weight}} = 1106\) g, \(s_y = 43\) g
- \(\text{Cov}(x,y) = 753.119\)
questions.q: Calculate mean age, variance and std dev of age, and predicted weight at 37 months.
questions.explanation:
**Part a) Mean age:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{12776}{100} = 127.76 \text{ months}$$

**Part b) Variance & std dev:**
$$\text{Var}(x) = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{n} = \frac{54534}{100} = 545.34$$
$$s_x = \sqrt{545.34} \approx 23.35 \text{ months}$$

**Part c) Regression prediction:**
$$b = \frac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{\text{Var}(x)} = \frac{753.119}{545.34} \approx 1.381$$
$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 1106 - 1.381 \times 127.76 = 1106 - 176.40 = 929.60$$
$$\hat{y}(37) = 929.60 + 1.381 \times 37 = 929.60 + 51.10 = 980.70 \approx 980.66 \text{ g}$$

questions.context: A winery claims bottles contain 750 ml. A sample of \(n=10\) bottles gives \(\bar{x} = 749.8\) ml, \(s = 4.97\) ml. Test \(H_0: \mu = 750\) vs \(H_1: \mu \neq 750\) at \(\alpha = 5\%\).
questions.q: a) Perform the t-test: calculate the test statistic, critical value, and state the decision.
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Compute test statistic:
$$t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{749.8 - 750}{4.97/\sqrt{10}} = \frac{-0.2}{1.5712} \approx -0.1273$$

**Step 2:** Critical value (two-sided, \(df=9\), \(\alpha=0.05\)):
$$t_{9,\,0.975} = 2.262$$

**Step 3:** Compare:
$$|t| = 0.1273 < 2.262$$

**Decision:** Fail to reject \(H_0\). There is no significant evidence that the true mean fill differs from 750 ml.

questions.context: Winery study: \(n=1000\) bottles sampled. Proportion with defective corks \(\hat{p} = 0.10\).
questions.q: b) Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true proportion of defective corks.

questions.context: A hypothesis test is conducted. The computed p-value is 74.1% and the significance level is \(\alpha = 5\%\).
questions.q: c) What is the correct decision?
questions.explanation:
**Rule:** Reject \(H_0\) if \(p\text{-value} \leq \alpha\).

Here: \(p = 0.741 > 0.05 = \alpha\).

We **fail to reject \(H_0\)**. A p-value of 74.1% means the observed data are very consistent with the null hypothesis — there is no statistical evidence against \(H_0\).

title: Statistics Exercise Exam — SS2021 Group 2
description: Combinatorics, continuous random variables, probability, linear regression, normal distribution

questions.context: A continuous random variable \(X\) has PDF \(f(x) = \frac{k}{2} + kx\) for \(0 \leq x \leq 9\), and \(f(x)=0\) otherwise.
questions.q: Find k, the CDF F(x), and P(1 ≤ X ≤ 4).
questions.explanation:
**Part a)** Normalization:
$$\int_0^9\left(\frac{k}{2} + kx\right)dx = k\left[\frac{x}{2} + \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^9 = k\left(\frac{9}{2} + \frac{81}{2}\right) = k \cdot 45 = 1$$
$$\Rightarrow k = \frac{1}{45} \approx 0.0222$$

**Part b)** CDF:
$$F(x) = \int_0^x \left(\frac{1}{90} + \frac{t}{45}\right)dt = \frac{x}{90} + \frac{x^2}{90}$$

**Part c)**
$$P(1 \leq X \leq 4) = F(4) - F(1) = \left(\frac{4}{90} + \frac{16}{90}\right) - \left(\frac{1}{90} + \frac{1}{90}\right) = \frac{20}{90} - \frac{2}{90} = \frac{18}{90} = 0.2$$

questions.context: 3 friends and 7 other people (10 total) are seated in a row randomly.
questions.q: What is the probability that all 3 friends sit adjacent to each other (next to each other in a block)?
questions.explanation:
**Step 1:** Total arrangements: \(10!\)

**Step 2:** Favorable arrangements:
- Treat the 3 friends as 1 block → 8 entities to arrange: \(8!\) ways
- The 3 friends can arrange within their block: \(3!\) ways
- Favorable = \(8! \times 3!\)

**Step 3:** Probability:
$$P = \frac{8! \times 3!}{10!} = \frac{40320 \times 6}{3628800} = \frac{241920}{3628800} \approx 0.0667$$

questions.context: Consider the functions:\n$$f_1(x) = \frac{x}{2 + \sin(x^2)}, \qquad f_2(x) = \ln(3 \cdot 2^x)$$
questions.q: Differentiate \(f_1\) and \(f_2\). Evaluate both derivatives at \(x_0 = \sqrt{\pi}\).
questions.explanation:
**Differentiating \(f_1\) (Quotient Rule + Chain Rule):**
$$f_1'(x) =  rac{(2+\sin(x^2)) \cdot 1 - x \cdot \cos(x^2)\cdot 2x}{(2+\sin(x^2))^2} =  rac{2+\sin(x^2) - 2x^2\cos(x^2)}{(2+\sin(x^2))^2}$$

**Evaluate at \(x_0 = \sqrt{\pi}\):**
- \(\sin((\sqrt{\pi})^2) = \sin(\pi) = 0\)
- \(\cos(\pi) = -1\)
- Numerator: \(2 + 0 - 2\pi\cdot(-1) = 2 + 2\pi\)
- Denominator: \((2+0)^2 = 4\)
$$f_1'(\sqrt{\pi}) =  rac{2 + 2\pi}{4} =  rac{1+\pi}{2}$$

**Differentiating \(f_2\) (Logarithm rules):**
$$f_2(x) = \ln(3) + \ln(2^x) = \ln(3) + x\ln(2)$$
$$f_2'(x) = \ln(2) \quad 	ext{(constant)}$$

**Evaluate at \(x_0 = \sqrt{\pi}\):**
$$f_2'(\sqrt{\pi}) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

*Alternative (Taylor expansion):* \(\cos(x) = 1 - x^2/2 + O(x^4)\), so \( rac{\cos(x)-1}{x^2} =  rac{-x^2/2 + O(x^4)}{x^2} 	o - rac{1}{2}\).

$$ oxed{\lim_{x	o 0} rac{\cos(x)-1}{x^2} = - rac{1}{2}}$$

**Part b)** Check if direct substitution works:

eq 0\)

Since denominator \(
eq 0\), the limit is simply \(0/4 = 0\):

questions.context: Consider \(f(x) = xe^{-x}\) on \([0, \infty)\).
questions.q: a) Find all local and global extrema of \(f\).\nb) Determine the intervals of monotonicity (increasing/decreasing).\nc) Find inflection points and intervals of convexity/concavity.
questions.explanation:
**Derivatives:**
$$f'(x) = e^{-x} - xe^{-x} = (1-x)e^{-x}$$
$$f''(x) = -e^{-x} - (1-x)(-e^{-x}) = e^{-x}(-1+1-x) = (x-2)e^{-x}$$

**Part a) Extrema:**
- Critical point: \(f'(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=1\)
- \(f(1) = e^{-1} = 1/e \approx 0.368\)
- \(f(0) = 0\) (boundary)
- As \(x	o\infty\): \(f(x)	o 0\)
- \(f''(1) = (1-2)e^{-1} = -e^{-1} < 0\) → **local maximum** at \(x=1\), \(f(1)=1/e\)
- **Global max** at \(x=1\), value \(1/e\)
- **Global min** at \(x=0\), \(f(0)=0\) (on \([0,\infty)\))

**Part b) Monotonicity:**
- \(f'(x) > 0\) when \(x < 1\) → **increasing on \([0, 1)\)**
- \(f'(x) < 0\) when \(x > 1\) → **decreasing on \((1, \infty)\)**

**Part c) Convexity:**
- \(f''(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x=2\) → **inflection point** at \(x=2\), \(f(2)=2e^{-2}\)
- \(f''(x) < 0\) for \(0 < x < 2\) → **concave (convex downward)** on \((0,2)\)
- \(f''(x) > 0\) for \(x > 2\) → **convex (convex upward)** on \((2,\infty)\)

Evaluate the definite integral:\n$$\int_0^\pi x^2 \sin(x)\, dx$$
questions.explanation:
**Apply integration by parts twice.** Let \(u = x^2\), \(dv = \sin(x)dx\):

**First step:**
$$\int_0^\pi x^2\sin(x)\,dx = \left[-x^2\cos(x)ight]_0^\pi + 2\int_0^\pi x\cos(x)\,dx$$
$$= (-\pi^2\cos(\pi) + 0) + 2\int_0^\pi x\cos(x)\,dx = \pi^2 + 2\int_0^\pi x\cos(x)\,dx$$

**Second step** (by parts again, \(u=x\), \(dv=\cos(x)dx\)):
$$\int_0^\pi x\cos(x)\,dx = \left[x\sin(x)ight]_0^\pi - \int_0^\pi \sin(x)\,dx = 0 - \left[-\cos(x)ight]_0^\pi$$
$$= -[\cos(\pi) - \cos(0)] = -[-1-1] = 2$$

**Combine:**
$$\int_0^\pi x^2\sin(x)\,dx = \pi^2 + 2\cdot(-2) = \pi^2 - 4$$

questions.context: Consider the function \(h(x) = e^{\pi i x}\) on \([0, 1)\), treated as a \(1\)-periodic function.
questions.q: Compute the Fourier coefficients \(c_n\) of \(h\). State where the Fourier series converges.
questions.explanation:
**Fourier coefficients** (complex exponential basis \(e^{2\pi inx}\)):
$$c_n = \int_0^1 h(x)\,e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx = \int_0^1 e^{\pi ix}\cdot e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx = \int_0^1 e^{\pi i(1-2n)x}\,dx$$

**For \(1 - 2n 
eq 0\)** (i.e., \(n 
eq 1/2\), which is always true for integer \(n\)):
$$c_n = \left[ rac{e^{\pi i(1-2n)x}}{\pi i(1-2n)}ight]_0^1 =  rac{e^{\pi i(1-2n)} - 1}{\pi i(1-2n)}$$

**Simplify numerator:** \(e^{\pi i(1-2n)} = e^{\pi i}\cdot e^{-2\pi in} = (-1)\cdot 1 = -1\)
$$c_n =  rac{-1-1}{\pi i(1-2n)} =  rac{-2}{\pi i(1-2n)} =  rac{2i}{\pi(1-2n)} =  rac{2}{\pi(2n-1)i} \cdot (-1) \cdot (-i^2)$$

Simplified: $$ oxed{c_n =  rac{-2}{\pi i(1-2n)} =  rac{2i}{\pi(2n-1)}}$$

**Convergence:** Since \(h(x) = e^{\pi ix}\) is infinitely differentiable (\(C^\infty\)) on \((0,1)\), the Fourier series converges uniformly on any compact subset of \((0,1)\). At the boundary (periodization creates a jump discontinuity at \(x=0\equiv x=1\)), the series converges to the average of the left and right limits.

questions.context: Consider\n$$f(x,y) = \frac{xy}{x - y} \quad \text{for } x \neq y$$
questions.q: Determine where \(f\) is partially differentiable. Compute the partial derivatives \(\partial f/\partial x\) and \(\partial f/\partial y\) wherever they exist.
questions.explanation:
**Domain:** \(f\) is defined and smooth wherever \(x 
eq y\). The function is not defined (or can be continuously extended) on the line \(x = y\).

**Partial derivative with respect to \(x\) (quotient rule):**
$$ rac{\partial f}{\partial x} =  rac{y(x-y) - xy\cdot 1}{(x-y)^2} =  rac{xy - y^2 - xy}{(x-y)^2} =  rac{-y^2}{(x-y)^2}$$

**Partial derivative with respect to \(y\) (quotient rule):**
$$ rac{\partial f}{\partial y} =  rac{x(x-y) - xy\cdot(-1)}{(x-y)^2} =  rac{x^2 - xy + xy}{(x-y)^2} =  rac{x^2}{(x-y)^2}$$

**Summary:**
- Both partial derivatives exist for all \((x,y)\) with \(x 
eq y\).
- \(f\) is totally differentiable on \(\{(x,y) : x 
eq y\}\).
- On the line \(x = y\), \(f\) is not defined, so partial differentiability fails there.
- Gradient: \(
abla f = \left(\dfrac{-y^2}{(x-y)^2},\, \dfrac{x^2}{(x-y)^2}ight)\)

questions.context: Define\n$$f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{x}\, e^{-1/x^2} & x \neq 0 \\ 0 & x = 0 \end{cases}$$
questions.q: Perform a complete curve sketch: (a) continuity and differentiability, (b) local and global extrema, (c) monotonicity, (d) sketch.
questions.explanation:
**Part a) Continuity:**
For \(x
eq 0\): \(f\) is a composition of smooth functions, hence \(C^\infty\).
At \(x=0\): \(\lim_{x	o 0^+}  rac{1}{x}e^{-1/x^2}\). Let \(t=1/x	o+\infty\): \(te^{-t^2}	o 0\). Similarly for \(x	o 0^-\). So \(f\) is **continuous everywhere**.

**Differentiability at \(x=0\):** \(f'(0) = \lim_{h	o 0} rac{f(h)}{h} = \lim_{h	o 0} rac{1}{h^2}e^{-1/h^2} = \lim_{t	o\infty}t^2e^{-t^2} = 0\). So \(f\) is **differentiable everywhere**.

**Part b) Derivative (for \(x
eq 0\)):**
$$f'(x) =  rac{d}{dx}\left( rac{1}{x}e^{-1/x^2}ight) = \left(- rac{1}{x^2} +  rac{2}{x^4}ight)e^{-1/x^2} =  rac{2-x^2}{x^4}\,e^{-1/x^2}$$

Critical points: \(2-x^2=0 \Rightarrow x = \pm\sqrt{2} = \pm rac{1}{\sqrt{0.5}}\).

Actually \(f'(x)=0\) when \(2 - x^2 = 0\), i.e., \(x = \pm\sqrt{2}\).
- Sign of \(f'\): positive for \(0 < x < \sqrt{2}\) (since \(2-x^2>0\)), negative for \(x > \sqrt{2}\)
- For \(x < 0\): \(x^4 > 0\) and \((2-x^2)\) is positive for \(|x|<\sqrt{2}\)
  - \(-\sqrt{2} < x < 0\): \(f'(x) > 0\) (increasing)
  - \(x < -\sqrt{2}\): \(f'(x) < 0\) (decreasing)

**Extrema:**
- **Local maximum** at \(x =  rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\): \(f\left( rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}ight) = \sqrt{2}\,e^{-2}\)
- **Local minimum** at \(x = - rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\): \(f\left(- rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}ight) = -\sqrt{2}\,e^{-2}\)
- No global extrema (\(f	o 0\) as \(x	o\pm\infty\) and \(f(0)=0\), so max/min are only local)

**Part c) Monotonicity:**
- Decreasing on \((-\infty, - rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}]\)
- Increasing on \([- rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0)\)
- Increasing on \((0,  rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}]\)
- Decreasing on \([ rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \infty)\)

**Part d) Sketch key features:**
- Antisymmetric: \(f(-x) = -f(x)\) (odd function)
- Passes through \((0,0)\); smooth bump above x-axis for \(x>0\), mirror below for \(x<0\)
- Max \(\approx 0.271\) at \(x\approx 0.707\)

Find antiderivatives (indefinite integrals) for:

a) \(\int xe^x\,dx\)

b) \(\int  rac{e^x}{e^x + 3}\,dx\)

c) Find \(F(x)\) with \(F'(x) = \cos(x^5 + \pi)\cdot 5x^4\) and \(F(0) = 42\).
questions.explanation:
**Part a)** Integration by parts: \(u=x\), \(dv=e^x dx\):
$$\int xe^x\,dx = xe^x - \int e^x\,dx = xe^x - e^x + C = (x-1)e^x + C$$

**Part b)** Substitution \(u = e^x + 3\), \(du = e^x\,dx\):
$$\int  rac{e^x}{e^x+3}\,dx = \int  rac{du}{u} = \ln|u| + C = \ln|e^x+3| + C = \ln(e^x+3) + C$$

**Part c)** Recognise \(F'(x) = 5x^4\cos(x^5+\pi)\). Let \(u = x^5+\pi\), \(du = 5x^4\,dx\):
$$F(x) = \int \cos(x^5+\pi)\cdot 5x^4\,dx = \sin(x^5+\pi) + C$$

Apply initial condition \(F(0) = 42\):
$$F(0) = \sin(0^5+\pi) + C = \sin(\pi) + C = 0 + C = C = 42$$

$$ oxed{F(x) = \sin(x^5+\pi) + 42}$$

questions.context: Minimize/maximize \(f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1+4)^2 + (x_2-4)^2\) subject to the constraint \(x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 8\).
questions.q: Find all global extrema of \(f\) on the closed disk \(x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 8\).
questions.explanation:
Geometrically, \(f(x_1,x_2)\) is the squared Euclidean distance from the point \((-4, 4)\) to \((x_1,x_2)\).

**Step 1: Interior check.**
\(
abla f = (2(x_1+4),\, 2(x_2-4)) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1=-4,\, x_2=4\).
Check: \((-4)^2+4^2=32 > 8\), so the unconstrained minimum is **outside** the feasible region.

**Step 2: Boundary (\(x_1^2+x_2^2=8\), i.e., circle of radius \(\sqrt{8}=2\sqrt{2}\)).**
The point \((-4,4)\) is at distance \(\sqrt{16+16}=4\sqrt{2}\) from the origin.

- **Closest point on boundary** to \((-4,4)\): along direction \( rac{(-4,4)}{4\sqrt{2}} = \left(- rac{1}{\sqrt{2}},  rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}ight)\); point: \(2\sqrt{2}\cdot\left(- rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}ight) = (-2, 2)\)
- **Farthest point** (diametrically opposite): \((2, -2)\)

**Step 3: Function values.**
- \(f(-2,2) = (-2+4)^2+(2-4)^2 = 4+4 = 8\) → **global minimum**
- \(f(2,-2) = (2+4)^2+(-2-4)^2 = 36+36 = 72\) → **global maximum**

**Conclusion:**
- Global minimum: \(f=8\) at \((-2, 2)\)
- Global maximum: \(f=72\) at \((2, -2)\)

questions.context: Let \(f: \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^3\) and \(g: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^3\) be given by:\n$$f(u,v,w) = (u^2,\, v^2,\, u-w), \qquad g(x,y) = (xy,\, x^2+y^2,\, \cos(x-y))$$\nConsider the composition \(F = f \circ g\).
questions.q: Compute the Jacobian matrix \(J_F\) at the point \((x,y) = \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)\) using the chain rule.
questions.explanation:
**Step 1: Evaluate \(g\left( rac12, rac12ight)\):**
$$g\left(	frac12,	frac12ight) = \left(	frac12\cdot	frac12,\; 	frac14+	frac14,\; \cos(0)ight) = \left(	frac14,\; 	frac12,\; 1ight)$$

**Step 2: Jacobian of \(f\) at \((u,v,w) = \left( rac14, rac12,1ight)\):**
$$J_f =  egin{pmatrix} 2u & 0 & 0 \ 0 & 2v & 0 \ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\Bigg|_{(1/4,\,1/2,\,1)} =  egin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 0 & 0 \ 0 & 1 & 0 \ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Jacobian of \(g\) at \(\left( rac12, rac12ight)\):**
$$g_1 = xy:\; \partial/\partial x = y = 1/2,\; \partial/\partial y = x = 1/2$$
$$g_2 = x^2+y^2:\; \partial/\partial x = 2x = 1,\; \partial/\partial y = 2y = 1$$
$$g_3 = \cos(x-y):\; \partial/\partial x = -\sin(x-y) = 0,\; \partial/\partial y = \sin(x-y) = 0$$
$$J_g =  egin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \ 1 & 1 \ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Chain rule \(J_F = J_f \cdot J_g\):**
$$J_F =  egin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 0 & 0 \ 0 & 1 & 0 \ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}  egin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \ 1 & 1 \ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} =  egin{pmatrix} 1/4 & 1/4 \ 1 & 1 \ 1/2 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix}$$

questions.q: a) Find an antiderivative \(F(x)\) of \(f(x) = xe^x\).\n\nb) Find \(F(x)\) with \(F'(x) = xe^x\) and \(F(0) = 2\).
questions.explanation:
**Part a) Integration by parts:** Let \(u=x\), \(dv=e^x\,dx\), so \(du=dx\), \(v=e^x\):
$$F(x) = \int xe^x\,dx = xe^x - \int e^x\,dx = xe^x - e^x + C = (x-1)e^x + C$$

**Part b) Apply initial condition \(F(0)=2\):**
$$F(0) = (0-1)e^0 + C = -1 + C = 2 \Rightarrow C = 3$$
$$ oxed{F(x) = (x-1)e^x + 3}$$

questions.q: Evaluate the definite integral:\n$$\int_0^{\sqrt{\pi/2}} x\cos\!\left(x^2 - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)dx$$
questions.explanation:
**Substitution:** Let \(u = x^2 -  rac{\pi}{2}\), so \(du = 2x\,dx\), i.e., \(x\,dx =  rac{1}{2}du\).

**New limits:**
- \(x=0 \Rightarrow u = -\pi/2\)
- \(x=\sqrt{\pi/2} \Rightarrow u = \pi/2 - \pi/2 = 0\)

**Integral:**
$$\int_0^{\sqrt{\pi/2}} x\cos(x^2-\pi/2)\,dx =  rac{1}{2}\int_{-\pi/2}^0 \cos(u)\,du =  rac{1}{2}\left[\sin(u)ight]_{-\pi/2}^0$$
$$=  rac{1}{2}(\sin(0) - \sin(-\pi/2)) =  rac{1}{2}(0 - (-1)) =  rac{1}{2}$$

questions.q: State the definition of the derivative \(f'(x_0)\) using limits.
questions.explanation:
**Definition:**
$$f'(x_0) = \lim_{h 	o 0}  rac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h}$$

provided this limit exists.

**Alternative form:**
$$f'(x_0) = \lim_{x 	o x_0}  rac{f(x) - f(x_0)}{x - x_0}$$

**Geometric interpretation:** The derivative gives the slope of the tangent line to the graph of \(f\) at the point \((x_0, f(x_0))\).

questions.q: Prove that the derivative of \(f(x) = x^n\) (for integer \(n \geq 1\)) is \(f'(x) = nx^{n-1}\) using the limit definition.
questions.explanation:
**From the definition:**
$$f'(x) = \lim_{h	o 0}  rac{(x+h)^n - x^n}{h}$$

**Apply the Binomial Theorem:**
$$(x+h)^n = \sum_{k=0}^n  inom{n}{k} x^{n-k}h^k = x^n + nx^{n-1}h +  inom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^2 + \cdots + h^n$$

**Subtract and divide by \(h\):**
$$ rac{(x+h)^n - x^n}{h} = nx^{n-1} +  inom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h + \cdots + h^{n-1}$$

**Take limit as \(h	o 0\):** All terms containing \(h\) vanish:
$$f'(x) = nx^{n-1} \qquad \square$$

questions.context: Consider the function \(f(x) = e^{x^3 - 12x}\).
questions.q: i) Find \(f'(x)\).\nii) Find all stationary points.\niii) Classify each stationary point using \(f''(x)\).
questions.explanation:
**Part i) Chain rule:**
$$f'(x) = e^{x^3-12x} \cdot (3x^2 - 12)$$

**Part ii) Stationary points** (\(f'(x)=0\)):
Since \(e^{x^3-12x} > 0\) always, set \(3x^2-12=0\):
$$3(x^2-4) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2$$

**Part iii) Second derivative:**
$$f''(x) = e^{x^3-12x}\cdot(3x^2-12)^2 + e^{x^3-12x}\cdot 6x = e^{x^3-12x}\left[(3x^2-12)^2 + 6xight]$$

At \(x=2\): \((3\cdot4-12)^2 + 12 = 0 + 12 = 12 > 0\), and \(e^{8-24} = e^{-16} > 0\):
$$f''(2) = 12e^{-16} > 0 \Rightarrow 	extbf{local minimum at } x=2$$

At \(x=-2\): \((12-12)^2 + 6(-2) = 0 - 12 = -12 < 0\), and \(e^{-8+24} = e^{16} > 0\):
$$f''(-2) = -12e^{16} < 0 \Rightarrow 	extbf{local maximum at } x=-2$$

questions.q: Define what it means for a sequence \((a_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\) to converge to a limit \(L\). State the formal \(\varepsilon\)-\(N\) definition.
questions.explanation:
**Definition (\(\varepsilon\)-\(N\) convergence):**

A sequence \((a_n)\) converges to \(L \in \mathbb{R}\), written \(\lim_{n	o\infty} a_n = L\), if:
$$ orall\, \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\, N \in \mathbb{N}\; 	ext{ such that }\;  orall\, n > N:\; |a_n - L| < \varepsilon$$

**Intuition:** For every desired precision \(\varepsilon > 0\), there is a point in the sequence after which all terms stay within distance \(\varepsilon\) of \(L\).

**Example:** Show \(a_n = 1/n 	o 0\).
Given \(\varepsilon > 0\), choose \(N > 1/\varepsilon\). Then for \(n > N\):
$$|a_n - 0| =  rac{1}{n} <  rac{1}{N} < \varepsilon \qquad \square$$

questions.q: Define continuity of a function \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) at a point \(x_0\) using the \(\varepsilon\)-\(\delta\) criterion. Prove that \(f(x) = 2x + 1\) is continuous at \(x_0 = 3\).
questions.explanation:
**Definition:** \(f\) is continuous at \(x_0\) if:
$$ orall\,\varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\,\delta > 0\; 	ext{ such that }\; |x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x) - f(x_0)| < \varepsilon$$

**Proof for \(f(x) = 2x+1\) at \(x_0=3\):**

\(f(x_0) = f(3) = 7\). We need \(|f(x) - 7| < \varepsilon\) whenever \(|x-3| < \delta\).

$$|f(x) - f(x_0)| = |(2x+1) - 7| = |2x - 6| = 2|x-3|$$

Given \(\varepsilon > 0\), choose \(\delta = \varepsilon/2\). Then:
$$|x-3| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(3)| = 2|x-3| < 2\cdot rac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon$$

Since \(\delta > 0\) exists for every \(\varepsilon > 0\), \(f\) is continuous at \(x_0=3\). \(\square\)

questions.context: Consider \(f(x) = \dfrac{x}{\ln(x)}\).
questions.q:
a) State the domain of \(f\).
b) Compute all relevant limits (as \(x	o 0^+\), \(x	o 1^\pm\), \(x	o\infty\)).
c) Find all stationary points and classify them.
d) Sketch the function.
questions.explanation:
**Part a) Domain:**
\(\ln(x)\) is defined for \(x > 0\), but we must exclude \(x=1\) (where \(\ln(1)=0\)).
$$	ext{Domain} = (0,1) \cup (1, \infty)$$

**Part b) Limits:**
- \(\lim_{x	o 0^+} f(x) = \lim_{x	o 0^+}  rac{x}{\ln x}\): numerator \(	o 0\), denominator \(	o -\infty\), so \(f(x)	o 0^-\) (from below)
- \(\lim_{x	o 1^-} f(x)\): \(x	o1\), \(\ln x	o 0^-\), so \(f(x)	o 1/0^- = -\infty\)
- \(\lim_{x	o 1^+} f(x)\): \(\ln x	o 0^+\), so \(f(x)	o +\infty\)
- \(\lim_{x	o\infty} f(x) = \infty\) (since \(x\) grows faster than \(\ln x\) in terms of divergence, but actually both go to \(+\infty\); by L'Hôpital: \( rac{x}{\ln x}\overset{H}{\sim} rac{1}{1/x}=x	o\infty\))

**Part c) Stationary points:**
$$f'(x) =  rac{\ln(x) \cdot 1 - x \cdot  rac{1}{x}}{(\ln x)^2} =  rac{\ln x - 1}{(\ln x)^2}$$

Set \(f'(x)=0\): \(\ln x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \ln x = 1 \Rightarrow x = e\).

- For \(x \in (1,e)\): \(\ln x < 1\), so \(f'(x) < 0\) (decreasing)
- For \(x > e\): \(\ln x > 1\), so \(f'(x) > 0\) (increasing)
- **Local (and global on \((1,\infty)\)) minimum at \(x=e\):** \(f(e) = e/1 = e\)

**Part d) Sketch features:**
- Vertical asymptote at \(x=1\) (\(-\infty\) from left, \(+\infty\) from right)
- Approaches 0 as \(x	o 0^+\) (from below, on \((0,1)\))
- On \((1,\infty)\): decreases to minimum \(e\) at \(x=e\), then increases to \(\infty\)

questions.q:
Evaluate:
a) \(\displaystyle\int_0^{1/3} 27\pi x^2 e^{3x}\,dx\)

b) \(\displaystyle\lim_{x	o 0}  rac{e^{2x} - 2\sin(x) - 1 - 2x^2}{\sin(\pi x) - \pi x}\)
questions.explanation:
**Part a)** Use integration by parts for \(\int x^2 e^{3x}\,dx\).

Let \(I = \int x^2e^{3x}\,dx\). Apply IBP twice:
- First: \(u=x^2\), \(dv=e^{3x}dx\):
$$I =  rac{x^2e^{3x}}{3} -  rac{2}{3}\int xe^{3x}\,dx$$
- Second: \(u=x\), \(dv=e^{3x}dx\):
$$\int xe^{3x}\,dx =  rac{xe^{3x}}{3} -  rac{e^{3x}}{9}$$
- Therefore:
$$I =  rac{x^2e^{3x}}{3} -  rac{2}{3}\left( rac{xe^{3x}}{3} -  rac{e^{3x}}{9}ight) = e^{3x}\left( rac{x^2}{3} -  rac{2x}{9} +  rac{2}{27}ight)$$

Evaluate \(27\pi\int_0^{1/3}x^2e^{3x}\,dx = 27\pi\left[e^{3x}\left( rac{x^2}{3}- rac{2x}{9}+ rac{2}{27}ight)ight]_0^{1/3}\):
- At \(x=1/3\): \(e^1\left( rac{1/9}{3}- rac{2/3}{9}+ rac{2}{27}ight) = e\left( rac{1}{27}- rac{2}{27}+ rac{2}{27}ight) =  rac{e}{27}\)
- At \(x=0\): \( rac{2}{27}\)
$$27\pi\left( rac{e}{27}- rac{2}{27}ight) = 27\pi\cdot rac{e-2}{27} = \pi(e-2)$$

**Part b)** Both numerator and denominator approach 0. Use Taylor expansions around \(x=0\):

Numerator: \(e^{2x} = 1+2x+2x^2+ rac{4x^3}{3}+ rac{2x^4}{3}+\cdots\)
\(2\sin(x) = 2x- rac{x^3}{3}+\cdots\)
\(e^{2x}-2\sin(x)-1-2x^2 = (1+2x+2x^2+ rac{4x^3}{3}+\cdots) - (2x- rac{x^3}{3}+\cdots) - 1 - 2x^2\)
\(=  rac{4x^3}{3}+ rac{x^3}{3}+O(x^4) =  rac{5x^3}{3}+O(x^4)\)

Denominator: \(\sin(\pi x) = \pi x -  rac{(\pi x)^3}{6}+\cdots\)
\(\sin(\pi x)-\pi x = - rac{\pi^3 x^3}{6}+O(x^5)\)

**Limit:**
$$\lim_{x	o 0} rac{5x^3/3 + O(x^4)}{-\pi^3x^3/6+O(x^5)} =  rac{5/3}{-\pi^3/6} =  rac{5}{3}\cdot rac{-6}{\pi^3} =  rac{-10}{\pi^3}$$

questions.context: Consider the symmetric matrix\n$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 5 & x-1 \\ 0 & x-1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$
questions.q: Find all values of \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) for which \(A\) is positive definite.
questions.explanation:
**Sylvester's criterion:** A symmetric matrix is positive definite iff all leading principal minors are positive.

**Minor 1:** \(D_1 = 1 > 0\) ✓ (always)

**Minor 2:** \(D_2 =  egin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \ 2 & 5 \end{vmatrix} = 5 - 4 = 1 > 0\) ✓ (always)

**Minor 3:** \(D_3 = \det(A)\). Expand along first row:
$$\det(A) = 1\cdot egin{vmatrix}5 & x-1 \ x-1 & 1\end{vmatrix} - 2\cdot egin{vmatrix}2 & x-1 \ 0 & 1\end{vmatrix} + 0$$
$$= (5 - (x-1)^2) - 2(2) = 5 - (x-1)^2 - 4 = 1 - (x-1)^2$$

Require \(D_3 > 0\):
$$1 - (x-1)^2 > 0 \Rightarrow (x-1)^2 < 1 \Rightarrow |x-1| < 1 \Rightarrow 0 < x < 2$$

$$ oxed{A 	ext{ is positive definite iff } x \in (0, 2)}$$
